title: Azure Logic Apps - Platform, Hosting Models, And Core Concepts
applies_to: Choosing the correct Logic Apps hosting model before implementation

confirmed_facts.fact: Azure Logic Apps is a cloud platform for creating and running automated workflows across cloud, on-premises, and hybrid systems with low-code and no-code tools.
Azure Logic Apps provides more than 1,400 prebuilt connectors.

Azure Logic Apps distinguishes built-in connector operations, which run natively with the Logic Apps runtime, from managed connector operations, which are Microsoft-managed connectors hosted in Azure.
Built-in connector availability differs between Consumption and Standard workflows, and some built-in connectors are available only in one hosting model.

A Consumption logic app resource supports a single workflow hosted in multitenant Azure Logic Apps, while a Standard logic app resource supports multiple workflows hosted in single-tenant Azure Logic Apps, App Service Environment v3, or a partially connected environment.
Standard logic apps support both stateful and stateless workflows inside one logic app resource.
Single-tenant Azure Logic Apps supports direct access to resources in Azure virtual networks and keeps workflow data in the same region where the logic app is deployed.

implementation_notes: Choose Consumption when you need the simplest multitenant, pay-per-execution model and only one workflow per resource is acceptable.
Choose Standard when you need single-tenant isolation, multiple workflows per resource, integrated virtual-network access, or more control over runtime and performance settings.
Treat built-in operations and managed connectors differently in architecture and security planning because they run in different places and have different connection behavior.
After choosing the hosting model, validate the connector strategy separately because built-in connector availability and behavior differ between Consumption and Standard.
caveats: Consumption and Standard have materially different deployment, hosting, networking, and billing behavior; do not treat them as equivalent runtimes.
Data residency expectations differ between multitenant and single-tenant models.
Feature selection should account for whether the required connector or operation is built-in, managed, or only available under a specific hosting model.
